solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canPartitionKSubsets(int[] nums, int k) {
        int totalSum = 0;
        for (int num : nums) totalSum += num;
        if (totalSum % k != 0) return false;
        int target = totalSum / k;
        Arrays.sort((Arrays.stream(nums).boxed().sorted(Collections.reverseOrder()).mapToInt(Integer::intValue)).toArray());
        if (nums[0] > target) return false;
        boolean[] used = new boolean[nums.length];
        return backtrack(nums, k, 0, 0, target, used);
    }

    private boolean backtrack(int[] nums, int k, int start, int currentSum, int target, boolean[] used) {
        if (k == 0) return true;
        if (currentSum == target) return backtrack(nums, k - 1, 0, 0, target, used);
        for (int i = start; i < nums.length; i++) {
            if (used[i]) continue;
            if (currentSum + nums[i] > target) continue;
            used[i] = true;
            if (backtrack(nums, k, i + 1, currentSum + nums[i], target, used)) return true;
            used[i] = false;
            if (currentSum + nums[i] == target) return false;
            while (i + 1 < nums.length && nums[i + 1] == nums[i]) i++;
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(k * 2^n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses backtracking to recursively attempt to form k subsets with equal sums. It first checks if the total sum is divisible by k. If not, it returns false. It then sorts the array in descending order to optimize pruning. The backtracking function tries to build subsets by selecting unused elements and backtracks if the current path cannot form a valid subset. Pruning is applied to skip duplicate elements and avoid unnecessary work.
